items: Open the Calendar app on macOS, or sign in to in a browser.
Right-click the calendar in the sidebar -> Share Calendar -> Public Calendar .
Copy the share link. It starts with , which is fine to paste as-is.

blocks.text: Any other public iCal feed
Pick Other (any public iCal URL) in the Provider dropdown. That covers university course schedules, lab seminar series, conference timetables, sports schedules, anything that ends in or starts with .

blocks.title: Secret URLs leak your whole calendar
blocks.text: Google's Secret address in iCal format grants read access to every event title, time, and note in that calendar to anyone who has the URL. Treat it like a password (e.g., don't paste it into Slack, a public repo, or a shared notes doc). ResearchOS keeps the URL in your private data folder.
After a calendar is connected
Each subscription becomes a row in the Connected list at the top of the panel. Here's what each row gives you.

items: The label you gave it, plus a small uppercase tag with the provider type.
The full ICS URL, truncated to one line.
A Last synced timestamp once ResearchOS has fetched the feed at least once.
A 5-swatch color strip on the left edge of the row. Click any swatch to recolor that calendar's events on the grid.
An On / Off checkbox to hide a calendar's events without removing the subscription.
A red Remove link to drop the subscription. A confirmation dialog reminds you that ResearchOS events aren't touched.

blocks.text: If a feed fails to fetch three times in a row (a usual sign the share URL expired or was revoked), ResearchOS stops auto-retrying it and the row shows Stopped syncing - the link may be broken or expired . Click Retry now after fixing the link, or remove the subscription and re-add it with a fresh URL. A one-off hiccup instead shows a quieter note that it will retry on its own.
How often feeds refresh

ResearchOS refetches each feed at most once every 15 minutes per browser session. The fetch goes through a small server proxy (the same Vercel function that bypasses CORS for Google, Outlook, and iCloud), and the proxy caches the response on its own edge cache for another 15 minutes. Back-to-back page loads don't hammer the source calendar's servers as a result.

blocks.title: Stale-while-revalidate
blocks.text: The edge cache also serves the previous response while fetching a fresh copy in the background (stale-while-revalidate, up to 1 hour). In the worst case an event you just added could take up to 75 minutes to appear (15 min client window + 60 min stale window). Hard-refreshing the browser tab bypasses the client-side window but not the edge cache.
If you just added an event in the source calendar and want it on the ResearchOS grid right now, hard-refresh the browser tab or wait out the 15-minute window.
What URLs the server proxy will fetch

The proxy is the small server endpoint that fetches each ICS URL on your behalf, because browsers can't fetch directly (Google doesn't set the right CORS headers). The proxy refuses a few classes of URL outright so it can't be used to scan internal infrastructure.

items: Anything that isn't (so no , no , no ). Plain is rejected outright for security. The prefix is rewritten to first, so iCloud URLs still work.
and the loopback range .
The private IPv4 ranges , , and .
Link-local and the IPv6 equivalents ( , , ).
Responses that don't start with . This catches the case where a calendar share URL redirects to an HTML sign-in page instead of returning iCal text.

blocks.text: If a paste fails with one of those errors, double-check that the URL is the public ICS variant of the calendar (not the regular web-app share link).
Recurring events

Repeating events (e.g., a weekly seminar) are expanded into one chip per occurrence inside a ±2-year window around today. That keeps a 20-year-long daily recurrence from spawning ~7,300 chips on the grid. If you scroll the calendar more than two years backward or forward, repeating events from external feeds will stop showing up until you refresh the page from a closer date.
Want to edit linked events from ResearchOS?

Linked calendars are read-only by design. To make changes, open the event in its source app (Google Calendar, Outlook, Apple Calendar). Your edit shows up in ResearchOS on the next 15-minute refresh. Earlier ResearchOS builds had an OAuth-backed two-way sync for Google and Outlook. That surface was removed (2026-05-14) in favor of keeping the integration simple and credential-free. The ICS subscription flow described here is the only supported path now.

entries.title: LabArchives
entries.summary: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks, and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.
entries.blurb: Import Offline Notebook ZIPs via the credential-free path; optional API path for online-only inline images.

blocks.text: Bring notebooks out of LabArchives and into ResearchOS as native, file-on-disk tasks, and recover the inline images that LabArchives keeps in the cloud, all without any API credentials.
The shape of the integration

LabArchives and ResearchOS sit at opposite ends of a hand-off, not in a live sync. LabArchives is your ELN of record while you're running experiments, and ResearchOS is where you bring those notebooks once you want them on disk in a structured form. There's no two-way connection, and ResearchOS never writes back to LabArchives.
Everything runs through one entry point under Settings -> LabArchives .

items: Import from LabArchives is the import wizard. You download an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives and feed it to the wizard. Every notebook page becomes a ResearchOS task, and every folder becomes a project you can map to your existing project list. No credentials, works offline.

blocks.text: Form-A vs Form-B inline images

LabArchives stores inline images in two ways internally. The legacy path, called Form-A , embeds the image as a binary file inside the notebook page entry itself. The newer path, Form-B , uploads the image to LabArchives' cloud and leaves only a URL behind in the entry body.

When you generate an Offline Notebook export ZIP, only the Form-A images come along. The Form-B URLs are still in the entry text, but the image bytes were never bundled. In a typical recent notebook that's roughly half the inline images.

If you import the ZIP without fetching Form-B images, the wizard writes a placeholder for each one. Your notes still come across, and the images at those URLs aren't there yet. You can recover them at any time using one of the three paths described below, or remove individual placeholders using the broken-image popup.
When images are recovered, inline images land in the task's folder and the markdown is rewritten to point at the local copy. File attachments (non-image uploads) land in .

blocks.title: Skip image fetch on the first pass
blocks.text: It's fine to skip image recovery during the initial import. Form-B images become placeholders you can clean up later. The broken-image popup in any imported note lets you handle them one at a time, and the per-task "Rehydrate images" panel lets you batch them all at once.
Exporting an Offline Notebook ZIP from LabArchives
The ZIP that ResearchOS imports is LabArchives' built-in Offline Notebook export. Both the import and any image-recovery run depend on having this file first.
Running the import

items: Generate browser script is the DevTools-script path (see below).
Drop your own images is the manual folder/ZIP drop path (see below).

blocks.title: What ends up on disk
blocks.text: Each notebook page becomes a directory under with a body alongside a folder that holds a subfolder for file attachments, an subfolder for inline images, and an for provenance. Tasks are marked complete, with and set to the page's newest entry timestamp.

blocks.title: Importing the same notebook twice
blocks.text: Importing a notebook you've already brought over is safe. Pages that haven't changed are recognized and left alone. If a page was edited in LabArchives after your last import (new entries, or an existing entry updated), the Preview step flags it and lets you tick which changed pages to overwrite. Overwriting replaces that task's notes and attachments while keeping the task itself, so its id, name, project, dates, and any sharing stay intact. Nothing is overwritten unless you tick it.
Recovering Form-B images

Three paths exist to pull Form-B images into your notes. All three are credential-free, so ResearchOS never asks for your LabArchives password. The wizard hides its Fetch images step in demo mode, but the per-task Rehydrate panel and these recovery paths still work there, since they all run client-side against files you bring in.
Path 1, the DevTools browser script

The Generate browser script tab (in the wizard's Fetch images step, or in the per-task Rehydrate panel) shows a self-contained JavaScript snippet you paste into the browser console while you're logged into LabArchives. Here's what the script does.

items: Checks that it's running on . It refuses to do anything on other origins.
Fetches each missing image URL using your existing session cookies (no password, no API key, only the session the browser already has from your login).
Packages all successful fetches into a single ZIP and triggers one browser download.

blocks.text: Drop the downloaded ZIP into the Drop your own images tab (or drag it straight into the panel) and ResearchOS matches filenames automatically.

blocks.title: Security posture of the script
blocks.text: The script runs entirely in your browser. ResearchOS never sees the image bytes until you explicitly drop the resulting ZIP back into the wizard. The URLs are hard-coded into the script (no dynamic eval, no network round-trip back to ResearchOS). The flag only attaches cookies the browser already has from your LabArchives login, so the script cannot fetch anything you couldn't load by clicking the image yourself.
Path 2, a manual folder or ZIP drop

If you already have the Form-B image files on disk (from a previous download, a screenshot batch, or an export from another tool), use the Drop your own images tab. Drop a folder or a containing the image files and ResearchOS matches them to the expected filenames automatically. No script needed.

This path also accepts the ZIP produced by the DevTools script above, which is why the two-tab flow is designed the way it is. Generate and download in the DevTools tab, then drop the result in the manual tab.
Path 3, the per-image broken-placeholder popup
After import, any task that still has unresolved Form-B images shows broken image placeholders in the Lab Notes editor. Clicking a broken placeholder opens a small popup with three options.

items: Find on LabArchives opens the original image URL in a new tab pointed at . If you're logged in, the image loads and you can right-click and Save. Then use Replace from disk below, or drag the saved file onto the note.

Replace from disk opens a file picker. Pick a local image file, and ResearchOS writes it to , rewrites the markdown reference, and removes the entry from the task's sidecar so the popup doesn't re-appear on the next open.
Remove reference deletes the broken placeholder from the markdown entirely. Use this when you don't have the image and don't need it.

blocks.title: Drag-and-drop shortcut
blocks.text: If you saved an image via "Find on LabArchives" and it filename-matches an outstanding placeholder, dragging it straight onto the note triggers the same recovery pipeline as the file picker, no need to open the popup first.

entries.title: Security
entries.summary: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.
entries.blurb: What stays on your computer, what briefly touches our server, and what we never collect.

blocks.text: Your research data stays on your computer. We never see it. This page is the plain-English version of what that means in practice, where the narrow exceptions are, and how you can verify the claim for yourself.
The claim

ResearchOS is built as a browser-only app that reads and writes a folder you pick on your own machine. Your notes, results, project and task data, images, attachments, and calendar subscriptions all stay in that folder. Nothing about your work is uploaded to a database we control, because there is no database we control. A couple of narrow proxy routes we wrote exist for browser CORS reasons and are documented below, but they are streams, not stores. Vercel Web Analytics adds one outbound destination, also documented below.
What stays on your computer

blocks.title: The short version
blocks.text: Open your data folder in Finder or Explorer. Every experiment, note, image, and attachment you have written is sitting there as a regular file you can read. ResearchOS writes to that folder and nowhere else. Quit the app and your data stays exactly where it is.

When you pick a data folder, ResearchOS reads and writes everything directly to that folder on your machine. The bytes never leave it. You can open the folder at any time in your operating system's file browser and see exactly what is there.
Here is what the folder holds.

items: Every experiment, lab note, and result you write.
Every image, PDF, and arbitrary attachment you drop into a note.
The JSON for projects, tasks, dependencies, methods, and PCR protocols.
Your calendar subscription URLs and the events they pulled in.
The Argon2id-protected credentials in guarding your per-user profile.

blocks.text: {` / .gitignore app-managed, auto-appended for sensitive sidecars users/ _user_metadata.json cross-user color preferences + display names _global_counters.json cross-user id allocator public/ methods + PCR protocols shared across all users lab/ shared lab-account state (funding accounts, etc.) / settings.json user settings, default views, tab visibility _account.json Argon2id-protected credentials (replaces _auth.json) _counters.json id counters per entity type _shared_with_me.json inbound sharing pointers _calendar-feeds.json subscribed iCal URLs _notifications.json bell-dropdown rows _history/ per-record version history (.jsonl append logs) task/ .jsonl task_notes/ .jsonl task_results/ .jsonl project/ .jsonl notes/ .jsonl sequences/ .jsonl molecules/ .jsonl inventory_items/ .jsonl projects/ .json one flat file per project tasks/ .json one flat file per task (the JSON carries fields; long-form text and attachments live under results/task- / below) methods/ .json reusable protocols pcr_protocols/ .json PCR programs and recipes lc_gradients/ .json LC gradient programs cell_culture_schedules/ .json plate_layouts/ .json notes/ .json shared lab notes sequences/ .json DNA/RNA/protein sequences molecules/ .json chemical structures inventory_items/ .json inventory catalog entries datahub/ .json Data Hub datasets phylo/ .json phylogenetic tree files figures/ .json figure composer artboards results/task- / per-task long-form content + attachments notes.md your Lab Notes tab writeup results.md your Results tab writeup notes/Images/ images dropped into Lab Notes tab notes/Files/ files dropped into Lab Notes tab results/Images/ images dropped into Results tab results/Files/ files dropped into Results tab events/ .json native calendar events goals/ .json project goals lab_links/ .json link library entries purchase_items/ .json purchase orders inbox/Images/ photos awaiting routing`}
What briefly touches a server we operate

A few routes on the ResearchOS server are involved. Two are CORS-bypass streams that exist because browsers refuse to talk directly to the upstream service. One is the optional AI assistant. The last two are anonymous telemetry, a page-view ping and a feature-usage beacon. None of them ever sees the contents of your data folder.

items: Calendar feed sync. When you subscribe to an iCal URL (Google, Outlook, iCloud, a university calendar), the browser asks to fetch the iCal text from the upstream and stream it back. The subscription URL travels in the request header rather than in the URL query string, so it is never written to Vercel access logs. A 15-minute edge cache keeps repeated polls from hammering the upstream. We do not persist the URL or the contents.

ORCID publication lookup. When you view a researcher profile that lists an ORCID iD (during sharing or profile setup), the browser asks to fetch that person's public publication list from the ORCID API and hand it back. ORCID's API isn't CORS-open, so the call has to go server-side. The route only ever touches public ORCID data, it's rate-limited per IP, and it stores nothing.

BeakerBot AI assistant. When you send a message to the built-in AI assistant (BeakerBot), the browser calls , which forwards the conversation to the Fireworks inference API and streams the response back. The route is only active when is on. It stores nothing server-side. No message content, no conversation history, and no file contents are persisted on our servers. Your local records are read on your machine and bundled into the request entirely in the browser before the call is made.

Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, your browser sends an anonymous page-view beacon to Vercel telling them which route you visited. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies, no project names. Vercel sees your IP address (which they hash before storage per their privacy policy). The Settings -> Offline mode toggle disables it durably, the script tag is never injected when offline mode is on.

Feature-usage beacon. When you use a tracked feature like sending a share or publishing a directory profile, the browser fires a small anonymous beacon to so the operator dashboard can count how often features get used. The payload is allow-listed enum and boolean flags only (was an ORCID present, did the share go to an existing user), never an ID, name, title, or anything you typed. It rides the same Offline mode gate, and the server re-validates and stores only the allow-listed shape.

blocks.text: The calendar CORS-bypass proxy uses the most defensive shape we know how to write, with HTTPS only, private-IP blocking, redirect re-validation, byte cap, timeout, content-type denylist, and per-IP rate limiting. The Vercel Analytics endpoint is a Vercel-owned script and beacon target, its posture is Vercel's, not ours. The route-defense code is in and if you want to read it line by line.
What we collect, and what we don't

We collect anonymous page-view pings via Vercel Web Analytics. When you navigate between pages, anonymous beacons go to Vercel. No IDs, no folder contents, no typed text, no markdown bodies. We use this to know which pages get used and which sit idle. Settings -> Offline mode disables it durably, the script is not injected when the toggle is on, and the toggle is read at component-mount time so the choice survives reloads.

We also collect anonymous feature-usage counts via , such as how often a share gets sent. These carry allow-listed enum and boolean flags only, never an ID, name, or anything you typed, and they ride the same Offline mode gate.

We do not collect anything else. No Sentry, no Google Analytics, no Mixpanel, no PostHog, no Hotjar, no Datadog, no Amplitude. No background phone-home. No crash reporter. No content telemetry. Running against the repo will confirm only is present, and the network tab will confirm no other endpoints are contacted.

The Report an issue button does not auto-submit anything. When you click it, your browser opens a pre-filled GitHub issue URL in a new tab. You see the body, edit it, and click Submit . Nothing happens until you do.

blocks.alt: The Report an Issue modal, showing the Type radio group, an editable Title field, an editable description, optional auto-attached error details, and the Cancel / Copy Link / Create GitHub Issue buttons at the bottom.
blocks.caption: The Report an Issue modal. You see the body, you edit the body, you choose whether to submit.

blocks.text: Honest limits worth knowing about
These claims hold, but they have implications worth understanding before you trust ResearchOS with anything sensitive.

blocks.title: Folder sharing means folder trust
blocks.text: If you share your data folder with a lab-mate over OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, or iCloud, every byte in it is theirs to read. The per-user password gate stops accidental account-switching inside the app, not someone opening the folder in their own file browser.

blocks.title: Passwords aren't encryption
blocks.text: Account credentials are protected with Argon2id (t=3, m=64 MiB), stored in , so a snooping co-located user cannot trivially extract them, but the research data itself is stored as plain JSON on disk. Anyone with direct disk access to the folder can read it. Turn on OS-level full-disk encryption (FileVault on macOS, BitLocker on Windows) if your laptop walks around.

blocks.title: Public hosting is opt-in, and the proxy is open
blocks.text: If you self-host ResearchOS on a public Vercel deploy with no auth gate, anyone on the internet can hit the calendar-feed proxy route (subject to rate limiting). This is not a data-exfiltration risk because the proxy has no access to your data folder, but it does mean someone could burn your Vercel function budget. Set both and for shared-state rate limiting on public deploys (the Vercel Upstash integration provisions both). The wiring is already in place.

blocks.title: Live collaboration uploads a readable copy
blocks.text: One-time sends to someone outside your folder are end-to-end encrypted, the relay holds only ciphertext and we cannot read it. Live real-time collaboration is the exception. When you co-edit a note or a shared notebook live, the shared document is synced to our servers in readable form so every change reaches the other person right away, which means we can read what you co-edit there. Anything you do not put into a live shared document stays on your machine and is never uploaded.
How to verify it yourself
You don't have to take any of this on faith. There are two ways to confirm what the app is doing. The first is built into Settings and takes about thirty seconds.

blocks.title: The easy way is to open Settings
blocks.text: ResearchOS ships three affordances inside Settings. The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English. The Offline mode toggle disables the proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics script tag in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface. "Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.

items: The Data inventory panel lists every file path the app has written and every IndexedDB key in use, alongside an External Calls section that names each outbound endpoint in plain English.
The Offline mode toggle disables the proxy routes plus the Vercel Analytics script tag in one click, for anyone who wants zero outbound network from the app surface.
"Where is this stored?" hints on each integration field so you always know which file holds the credential you just entered.

blocks.alt: The Data inventory panel in Settings, listing every file path the app has written and the External Calls section naming each outbound endpoint.
blocks.caption: Data inventory panel in Settings. Every file path and every outbound endpoint is listed here.

blocks.alt: The Offline mode toggle in Settings, shown in the on position, with the note that the Vercel Analytics script and proxy routes are disabled.
blocks.caption: Offline mode toggle in Settings. One click disables the analytics script and all proxy routes.

blocks.text: Your browser already shows every network request the app makes. This is the audit-grade path for anyone who wants to see the bytes themselves.

items: holds the opaque File System Access handle for your data folder. This is what gives the browser permission to read and write the folder you picked.
holds three small session-routing strings, the folder name plus its grant timestamp, the currently signed-in user, and (if you are a PI signed in) the primary account.

blocks.text: What we just fixed

ResearchOS went through an internal security audit on 2026-05-15. It closed one Critical finding (cross-site scripting via unsanitized markdown HTML across all 8 markdown rendering sites in the app) and several Important findings around the proxy routes, the LabArchives credential flow, and the in-app verification surface (data inventory, offline mode, storage hints). The audit report is checked into the repo as SECURITY_AUDIT.md , and the merge batch on sits around (audit doc) and (XSS sanitize + CSP).
If you find something that looks wrong, open a GitHub issue (the Report an issue button is the fastest path) or email the maintainer directly. We'd much rather hear about a problem early.
Atomic-write safety

A subtler kind of data loss has nothing to do with the network and everything to do with timing. If an app is halfway through writing a file when the tab closes, the laptop sleeps, or the process crashes, a naive implementation can leave the file zero-byte or truncated, and the good version you had a second ago is gone. ResearchOS is built so that cannot happen.

Every save writes to a temporary file first, lets that file finish landing durably on disk, and only then atomically moves it into place over the real file. The move is the kind of operation that either fully happens or does not happen at all, with no in-between state on disk. So if a crash interrupts a save, the worst case is that your previous good version survives untouched. You can never be left with a half-written or empty file in place of your data. The implementation is in if you want to read the exact sequence.

blocks.title: The worst case is your last good save
blocks.text: A torn write can only ever leave the old file contents intact, never a corrupt or empty one. The atomic move is the whole point. Either the new version replaces the old one cleanly, or the old one is still there.
Tested on every commit

A trust claim is only as good as the thing that keeps it true over time. The whole app is gated by automated tests that run on every commit and every pull request to . That gate runs linting, a full TypeScript typecheck, the Vitest unit and integration suite with coverage, and Playwright end-to-end tests in a real browser. The workflow lives in . If any of those gates fail, the change does not ship. That same machinery is what keeps the scientific calculations honest, which is its own page. Method validation explains how every sequence and lab calculation is re-checked against the reference tools the field trusts, on every commit.

entries.title: Shared Lab Accounts
entries.summary: One folder, shared across your whole lab. Everyone keeps their own data, and the PI's dashboard rolls it all up.
entries.blurb: Run one ResearchOS folder across a whole lab.

blocks.text: One folder, shared across your whole lab. Everyone keeps their own data, and the PI's dashboard rolls it all up.

blocks.title: Want to feel out the shared model first?
blocks.text: Open Demo Mode for a no-setup preview. It seeds two researchers (alex and morgan) plus their PI (Mira) into one shared folder, so cross-user data and the PI rollup are already wired up for you to click around in.

blocks.title: Lab accounts via ResearchOS cloud
blocks.text: If your lab uses ResearchOS cloud accounts, the PI creates a lab and invites members by email. Members accept and the lab covers their storage from the shared pool. No shared folder is required for this path. See Accounts and tiers for the invite and join flow. The shared-folder setup below is the alternative for labs that prefer to manage their own sync through OneDrive, Dropbox, Google Drive, or iCloud.
How it works

Put your ResearchOS folder inside a cloud-synced folder (e.g., OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, or iCloud). Every lab member points ResearchOS at that same folder on their own computer. Inside the folder, each member picks a different username, so their data lives at , , and so on.
The cloud provider handles syncing files between machines. ResearchOS has no idea any of this is happening, it just reads and writes JSON files like normal.

blocks.title: The one rule, keep the folder available offline
blocks.text: ResearchOS NEEDS to read and write the folder directly on disk. If your cloud provider keeps the folder "online only" (i.e., a placeholder file that downloads on demand), writes will fail or be silently dropped. EVERY member needs to flip the "always keep local" switch for the lab folder on EVERY laptop they use ResearchOS from.
Setup steps (every member runs these once)
Pick your cloud provider
Verify the folder is local
Before you start using ResearchOS, open the shared folder in Finder (macOS) or Explorer (Windows) and confirm:

items: Files show as fully downloaded (i.e., no cloud icon, no "online only" badge, no "available when online" tag).
You can open a sample file with the network disconnected.
The folder's "Size on disk" in its properties dialog is non-zero and roughly matches the "Size".

blocks.title: The PI sees the lab roll-up
blocks.text: Once everyone is set up, the PI sees a customizable dashboard at with widgets for cross-lab activity, purchases, member workload, announcements, and more. See Lab Overview . If you only want one labmate's work surfaced on your own Home page (rather than the whole lab), they can share an individual project or task with you instead; see Home & Projects .
Pitfalls to avoid

items: Don't put the folder in two different cloud providers. Pick one. Stacking OneDrive and Dropbox on the same folder corrupts JSON files when both try to write.
Don't use the same username on two laptops. If Sarah signs in as on her laptop and also on a shared lab computer at the same time, the two ResearchOS sessions will overwrite each other's files.
Don't edit files outside ResearchOS while the app is open. The app caches data in memory, so external edits won't be picked up until you reload the page.

entries.title: Box
entries.summary: Box Drive streams files on demand. Mark the ResearchOS folder Available Offline so it stays on disk.
entries.blurb: Box Drive with Make Available Offline.

blocks.text: Box Drive streams files on demand. Mark the ResearchOS folder Available Offline so it stays on disk.
Install Box Drive

Download Box Drive from box.com/resources/downloads . You need the desktop client running. The web interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.

blocks.title: Box Drive, not Box Sync
blocks.text: Box Sync is the older client and has been retired. Make sure you're installing Box Drive . Box Drive shows every folder you have access to as a virtual filesystem and downloads files on demand, whereas Box Sync mirrored selected folders to disk. The instructions below assume Box Drive.
Share the lab folder
Make it Available Offline (every member, every laptop)

Box Drive streams files on demand by default, so the folder shows up as a placeholder until you open something. ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still cloud-only breaks. You need to mark the lab folder as available offline on every laptop.

blocks.title: Folder picker may be slow
blocks.text: The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for Box folders. This is normal; Box Drive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

blocks.title: Enterprise policy can block Make Available Offline
blocks.text: Some Box Enterprise admins disable Make Available Offline for compliance reasons. If you right-click the folder and don't see the option, or it's greyed out, ask your IT admin to enable offline access for your account. Without it, ResearchOS will hit empty placeholder files instead of real content.
Where the folder lives on disk

items: Windows : under (e.g. ).
macOS : under on recent macOS versions, or on older installs.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls

items: Files revert to cloud-only. If a colleague opens the shared folder's properties and chooses Free up space , the folder switches back to streaming and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Make Available Offline .

Conflict files. If two members edit the same file while offline, Box creates a copy named next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.

Collaborator vs. owner storage. Shared folders on Box count against the folder owner's quota, not each collaborator's. Pick someone with enough quota (usually the lab's primary Box account) to own the folder.

Single sign-on re-auth. University Box accounts on SSO occasionally drop the Box Drive session and stop syncing without an obvious error. If files stop updating, open Box Drive's menu-bar / tray icon and confirm you're still signed in.

blocks.text: Box documentation

items: Installing Box Drive (Box Support)
Making files and folders available offline with Box Drive (Box Support)
Inviting collaborators to a folder (Box Support)

entries.title: Dropbox
entries.summary: Dropbox's Smart Sync can store files online-only. Set the ResearchOS folder to Local so it stays on disk.
entries.blurb: Smart Sync set to Local.

blocks.text: Dropbox's Smart Sync can store files online-only. Set the ResearchOS folder to Local so it stays on disk.
Install Dropbox
Download the desktop app from dropbox.com/install . You need the desktop client running. The web interface alone won't expose Dropbox as a folder ResearchOS can read.
Share the lab folder
Set Smart Sync to Local (every member, every laptop)

blocks.title: Smart Sync isn't on every plan
blocks.text: Smart Sync is included on Dropbox Plus, Family, Professional, and Business plans. On the free Basic plan, all files are local by default, so you can skip the Smart Sync step entirely. Just watch your storage quota.

blocks.title: Or keep all of Dropbox local
blocks.text: If you're on a paid plan and want every Dropbox folder to stay on disk by default, open the Dropbox menu-bar / tray icon → Preferences -> Sync -> New folder default and set it to Local . Existing folders keep their current setting; only newly-added folders inherit the new default. Pick this if you have multiple Dropbox folders besides the lab folder that all need to be always-local.
Common pitfalls

items: Default to Online-only. Some Business plans default new shared folders to Online-only . Check the team admin settings if Smart Sync keeps reverting.
Selective Sync vs. Smart Sync. Selective Sync hides the folder entirely from your disk, while Smart Sync keeps a placeholder. You want Smart Sync set to Local , not Selective Sync excluded.

LAN sync conflicts. If two members on the same network edit the same file in quick succession, Dropbox can produce "conflicted copy" files. These appear next to the original. Delete the conflicted copy in Finder / Explorer. ResearchOS only reads the canonical name.

blocks.text: Dropbox documentation

items: How to use Smart Sync (Dropbox Help)
Share files and folders (Dropbox Help)

entries.title: Google Drive
entries.summary: Install Drive for desktop and mark the lab folder Available offline. That keeps only the lab folder on disk without pulling your whole Drive local.
entries.blurb: Drive for desktop in Mirror mode.

blocks.text: Install Drive for desktop and mark the lab folder Available offline. That keeps only the lab folder on disk without pulling your whole Drive local.
Install Drive for desktop

Download Drive for desktop for Windows or macOS. The browser interface at is not enough. ResearchOS needs the desktop app because that's what creates the local filesystem folder you point ResearchOS at.
Share the lab folder
Mark the lab folder Available offline (every member, every laptop)

ResearchOS reads directly from disk, so anything still in Stream mode breaks. Most labs don't want the whole Drive switched to Mirror (your unrelated personal files, downloads, photos all get pulled local). Instead, mark just the lab folder as available offline.
Drive doesn't carry the "available offline" flag across machines, so every member has to do this on every laptop they use ResearchOS from.

blocks.title: Or switch the whole Drive to Mirror
blocks.text: If you want all of Drive locally (not just the lab folder), open Drive for desktop's Preferences → My Drive → Mirror files and confirm. Drive will download everything you have in My Drive over the next few minutes or hours depending on size. Pick this if you don't mind the extra disk use or you have other folders besides the lab folder that need to stay local.
Where the folder lives on disk

items: Windows : usually mounted as drive or under .
macOS : under or shown as a volume on the desktop.

blocks.text: Common pitfalls

items: Stream mode by accident. If files in the lab folder show a cloud icon or take seconds to open, the folder isn't marked offline yet. Right-click the lab folder and pick Available offline (or switch your whole Drive to Mirror if you prefer that).
"Shared with me" is not synced. A shared folder doesn't appear in your local Drive folder until you Add shortcut to Drive . Without that, ResearchOS has nothing to point at.
Workspace admins sometimes lock Mirror mode off. Check with your IT admin if Drive for desktop only shows the Stream option.

blocks.text: Google documentation

items: Use files offline with Drive for desktop (Google Help)
Share folders in Google Drive (Google Help)

entries.title: iCloud Drive
entries.summary: iCloud Drive Optimize Storage offloads unused files. Mark the ResearchOS folder Keep Downloaded so it stays local.
entries.blurb: Keep Downloaded on macOS.

blocks.text: iCloud Drive Optimize Storage offloads unused files. Mark the ResearchOS folder Keep Downloaded so it stays local.

blocks.title: macOS only, realistically
blocks.text: iCloud Drive technically runs on Windows via the iCloud for Windows app, but the experience is rough and Apple-specific permission quirks can break ResearchOS's folder access. If your lab has a mix of Mac and Windows users, we recommend OneDrive or Google Drive instead.
Enable iCloud Drive
On macOS, open System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud → iCloud Drive . Make sure it's turned on, and that the Desktop & Documents Folders sub-option is enabled if you plan to put the lab folder under .
Share the lab folder
Keep Downloaded (every member, every Mac)

blocks.title: Folder picker may be slow
blocks.text: The folder picker can take up to a minute to open for iCloud Drive folders. This is normal; iCloud needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

blocks.title: Or keep all of iCloud Drive local
blocks.text: If you'd rather not have macOS offloading anything in iCloud, open System Settings -> Apple ID -> iCloud and turn off Optimize Mac Storage . Every file in your iCloud Drive stays on disk from then on. Reasonable if you have plenty of disk space and other iCloud folders that also need to be always-local; the per-folder route above is the lighter option.
Common pitfalls

items: Optimize Mac Storage (System Settings → Apple ID → iCloud) will re-offload files macOS decides you haven't used recently. Keep Downloaded overrides this per folder, so make sure it's set on the lab folder, not just on individual files.
Permissions on shared collaborations are tracked by Apple ID. If someone's share is revoked, ResearchOS will see empty or unreadable files. Reaccept the share via the email link.
Hidden placeholder files appear if the file is online-only. If you see instead of , the file isn't local. Run Keep Downloaded again.

blocks.text: Apple documentation

items: Use iCloud Drive on your Mac (Apple Support)
Collaborate on folders with iCloud Drive (Apple Support)

entries.title: OneDrive
entries.summary: OneDrive's Files On-Demand keeps files in the cloud by default. You need to mark the ResearchOS folder Always keep on this device.
entries.blurb: Files On-Demand → Always keep on this device.

blocks.text: OneDrive's Files On-Demand keeps files in the cloud by default. You need to mark the ResearchOS folder Always keep on this device.
Get OneDrive

items: Windows 10/11 : pre-installed. Sign in via Settings → Accounts.
macOS : download from microsoft.com/onedrive/download .

blocks.text: Share the lab folder
Make it Always keep on this device (every member, every laptop)

blocks.title: Folder picker may be slow on Windows
blocks.text: The folder picker can take up to a minute to open on Windows for OneDrive folders. This is normal; OneDrive needs time to enumerate the folder tree. Wait for it rather than clicking again.

blocks.title: OneDrive Personal vs. Business / SharePoint
blocks.text: Shared folders sometimes show up as SharePoint libraries or in a separate "Shared with me" section. Microsoft's Add shortcut to My files button is the cleanest way to get the shared folder into your normal OneDrive tree, where Always keep on this device works reliably.

blocks.title: Or keep all of OneDrive local
blocks.text: If you'd rather have your whole OneDrive on disk (not just the lab folder), click the OneDrive icon in the menu-bar / tray → Settings -> Sync and backup -> Advanced settings -> Files On-Demand and switch to Download all OneDrive files now . Pick this if you have other OneDrive folders you also need available offline.
Common pitfalls

items: Storage Sense on Windows can auto-unpin files you haven't opened recently (i.e., move them back to cloud-only). Open Settings → System → Storage → Storage Sense and either disable the "content not opened in N days" option for the ResearchOS folder, or turn Storage Sense off entirely.
Cloud-only re-tag happens if you right-click and choose Free up space by accident. The folder reverts to cloud-only and ResearchOS can't read it. Re-run Always keep on this device .
Mac users : confirm the option says "Always keep on this device" (Files On-Demand). The older OneDrive Mac client stored files locally by default, but newer versions don't.

blocks.text: Microsoft documentation

items: OneDrive Files On-Demand (Microsoft Support)
Share OneDrive files and folders (Microsoft Support)

entries.title: Read this first
entries.summary: Yes, there are a lot of pages in here. Don't panic. Think of the wiki as a reference you dip into, not a book you read front to back. Come back here whenever you are stuck on something, find the page for it, skim, and get on with your day. This is the one page actually worth reading top to bottom.
entries.blurb: If you read one wiki page, read this one. What the app is, and the few things worth knowing before you start.

blocks.text: Yes, there are a lot of pages in here. Don't panic. Think of the wiki as a reference you dip into, not a book you read front to back. Come back here whenever you are stuck on something, find the page for it, skim, and get on with your day. This is the one page actually worth reading top to bottom.
It is short. It covers what ResearchOS is, the few things that will save you a headache later, and how to find everything else when you need it.
What ResearchOS is, in one paragraph

It is a research project manager that runs entirely in your browser and reads and writes a folder on your own computer. No upload required. You point it at a folder, pick a username, and everything you make (projects, tasks, lab notes, methods, protocols, purchases, attachments) gets saved as plain JSON and image files inside that folder. Optional Free and Lab cloud accounts add sync, sharing, and real-time collaboration. Put the folder in OneDrive, Dropbox, or iCloud and your whole lab can work out of the same one.
A few things worth knowing up front
Get these and you can pick up the rest as you go.

blocks.title: 1. Your data stays on your machine
blocks.text: Everything you do writes to the folder you connected. Nothing is uploaded, and there is no ResearchOS server holding your research. Want a backup? Copy the folder. Want to leave? Delete it. Deleting inside the app sends records to the Trash (the trash-can icon in the top-right), where you can restore them or delete them for good. The handful of network calls the app does make (fetching calendar feeds you opted into, the AI Helper when you turn it on, anonymous usage analytics) never carry your research, and the full story is on Security .

blocks.title: 2. The “?” button jumps you into this wiki
blocks.text: It is in the top-right of every page in the app. Click it and you land on the wiki page for whatever you were just looking at. Confused by a colored bar on the Gantt chart? Hit "?" and you are on the Gantt page.

blocks.title: 3. There is no home dashboard, you land where you work
blocks.text: Opening ResearchOS doesn't drop you on a dashboard to glance at. The root URL is just a router that sends you to the surface for your role . A member or solo researcher lands on the Workbench , opened to a grid of their projects, with its Projects, Experiments, Notes, Lists, and Check-ins tabs, a project filter that sticks as you move between them, and a popup holding the details, lab notes, method, and results. A PI lands on Lab Overview instead. Either way, you start on the thing you actually use.

blocks.title: 4. Tasks live in projects, projects live in your account
blocks.text: The shape is account, then projects, then tasks. Sharing is granular, you hand over one record at a time (most often a task, sometimes a method, note, project, or goal) and set read or edit per recipient. When you share a task with a labmate, it shows up in their Workbench under the project, editable if you let them. The rules are on Sharing and permissions .

blocks.title: 5. PIs get a Lab Overview surface
blocks.text: If your account is marked as a PI (Principal Investigator), you get Lab Overview , a curated page with cross-member views like member workload, recent activity, the purchase queue, and an audit log. Regular members never see it. Flip the PI flag on your account to get the bird's-eye view. More on PI .

blocks.title: 6. Calendar and the AI Helper are opt-in
blocks.text: ResearchOS works fine on its own. The calendar feeds (subscribe to a Google, Outlook, or iCloud calendar), the AI Helper, and the Companion phone pairing (the phone icon in the top-right) all live behind opt-in switches, off by default, and turn on independently. Settings sits behind your avatar in the top-right corner (click the circle with your initial), not in the top nav. Turn them on if and when you want them.
How to find the rest of the wiki
There are a lot of pages because the app does a lot. Three ways to get around, fastest first.

items: Search the bar at the top of the sidebar. If you know the word for the thing you want ("gradient," "calendar," "edit session"), this beats browsing every time.
Browse the sidebar categories. Pages are grouped under Getting Started, Shared Lab Accounts, Features, Integrations, and Security. Good when you know roughly what area you are in.
Hit the "?" button in the app. Drops you on the page for whatever you were just looking at. Best when you are mid-task and want context, not when you are browsing.

blocks.text: Every page leads with the concept. The top explains what the feature is and why it exists, and the screenshots and steps come after. Usually you can skim to the screenshot of the thing you need and stop there.
Brand new? Start here
Walk these in order. Each one is short.

items: Browser requirements . Chrome or Edge. The folder API does not work in Safari, Firefox, or Brave.
Connecting your folder . Pick the folder ResearchOS reads and writes.
Creating a user . A username, and an optional password.
Want a sandbox first? Try the demo . It runs in your browser on a fake yeast lab, and your edits vanish on reload.

blocks.text: Setting up a shared lab folder?

Read Shared Lab Accounts first. Putting the ResearchOS folder inside OneDrive, Google Drive, Dropbox, Box, or iCloud lets your whole lab work out of one folder. The catch is that you have to set the sync client to keep the folder downloaded locally instead of on-demand. Skip that and the app will fail to read or write at random, and you will hate it. The per-provider pages walk you through the exact setting.
One last thing

This is a local-first app built by one person, and the wiki tries to be honest about what works and what does not yet. If something is broken or confusing, the Feedback page in the app files a GitHub issue with the context already filled in. It is the fastest way to get it fixed.

entries.title: Reading your statistics
entries.summary: When the Data Hub runs a test, it hands back numbers and a one-line plain-language verdict. These pages explain what those numbers mean in the language a working scientist actually uses, so you can read your own result, write it up honestly, and know when to trust it. You do not need to be a statistician. You need to know what question each test answers and how to turn its output into a sentence.
entries.blurb: Plain-English guides to the numbers the Data Hub gives you: effect sizes, ANOVA, regression, survival, and more.

blocks.text: When the Data Hub runs a test, it hands back numbers and a one-line plain-language verdict. These pages explain what those numbers mean in the language a working scientist actually uses, so you can read your own result, write it up honestly, and know when to trust it. You do not need to be a statistician. You need to know what question each test answers and how to turn its output into a sentence.

blocks.title: How to use this section
blocks.text: Each page starts with what the test is and when a lab actually reaches for it, then shows you how to read the exact fields the Data Hub reports, with a worked example that turns the numbers into a sentence. Start with effect sizes and confidence intervals if you read only one. It is the idea every other page leans on.
Start here

Almost every result in the Data Hub reports a p-value , a number that tells you how surprising your data would be if nothing were really going on. A small p-value is a useful flag, but it does not tell you how big an effect is or whether it matters. For that you need the effect size and its confidence interval . That foundation page is worth reading before anything else, because every test below reports a version of the same three things, a direction, a size, and a range of uncertainty.

items: Effect sizes and confidence intervals , why the size of a difference matters more than a bare p-value, and how to read a 95% confidence interval.

blocks.text: Comparing groups

These tests answer "are these groups really different, and by how much?" Reach for them when you have a measured outcome (a fluorescence reading, a growth rate, a concentration) split across two or more conditions.

items: ANOVA, post-hoc tests, and two-way ANOVA , comparing three or more groups at once, then finding which specific pairs differ.
Repeated measures, mixed models, and nested designs , for when the same subjects are measured more than once, or your replicates are cells within mice rather than independent samples.

blocks.text: Relationships
These answer "do these two things move together, and can I predict one from the other?" Reach for them when both of your variables are measured numbers rather than groups.

items: Correlation and regression , measuring how tightly two variables track each other, and fitting a line you can read a slope off of.

blocks.text: Curves

Some biology is not a straight line. A drug saturates, a binding curve plateaus, an enzyme runs out of substrate. These pages cover fitting a shaped curve and reading the parameters that summarize it.

items: Dose-response curves , covering EC50 and IC50, the 4PL and 5PL sigmoid fits, the Hill slope, and comparing or sharing fits across datasets.

blocks.text: Survival and time-to-event

When your outcome is "how long until something happens," and some subjects have not had it happen yet, you need methods built for that. Reach for these for time to relapse, time to death, or time to any defined event.

items: Survival curves and hazard ratios , covering Kaplan-Meier curves, the log-rank test, and the Cox hazard ratio.

blocks.text: Counts and categories
When your data are counts in categories (responded or did not, mutant or wild type) rather than measured numbers, you compare proportions instead of means.

items: Contingency tables, odds ratios, and relative risk , covering chi-square and Fisher exact tests, plus the odds ratio that logistic regression also reports.

blocks.text: Screening and data quality
Two practical tools that sit alongside the rest, one for judging how well a measurement separates two groups, one for the honest question of whether a stray point is really an error.

items: ROC curves and AUC , how well a continuous measurement tells two groups apart, with sensitivity, specificity, and a cut point.
Outlier tests , the Grubbs test, and the honest caution that removing a data point needs a real reason.

blocks.text: Planning and assumption checking

Before you run a test, the power and sample-size planner lets you work out three questions in any direction: given an effect size, alpha, and a target power, how large does your sample need to be? Given the sample you already have, what power do you achieve? Given your sample and power target, what is the smallest effect you could detect? The planner covers the two-sample t-test, the paired t-test, one-way ANOVA, and Pearson correlation.

After you have data, the assumption Report Card runs the checks your chosen test relies on and surfaces them in plain language. For parametric tests it runs a Shapiro-Wilk normality test on each group and a Levene or Brown-Forsythe equal-variance test across groups, and tells you whether each assumption passes at your chosen alpha. A failing check is a prompt to look at a nonparametric alternative, not a verdict that the parametric result is wrong.

blocks.title: Every number is checked
blocks.text: Every test in these pages is validated against the tools labs already trust (scipy, statsmodels, lifelines, scikit-learn, and R) on the transparency page , which reruns the same inputs through those packages and shows the numbers line up. The result you read in the Data Hub is the same one those packages would give you.

entries.title: ANOVA, post-hoc tests, and two-way ANOVA
entries.summary: A t-test compares two groups. The moment you have three or more, you need ANOVA, which asks whether any of the groups differ before you go hunting for which ones. This page covers the one-way ANOVA, the post-hoc tests that find the specific pairs that differ, the two-way ANOVA for when two factors are in play at once, and the nonparametric alternatives for when normality does not hold.
entries.blurb: Compare three or more groups, find which pairs differ, and handle two factors at once.

blocks.text: A t-test compares two groups. The moment you have three or more, you need ANOVA, which asks whether any of the groups differ before you go hunting for which ones. This page covers the one-way ANOVA, the post-hoc tests that find the specific pairs that differ, the two-way ANOVA for when two factors are in play at once, and the nonparametric alternatives for when normality does not hold.
What ANOVA is

ANOVA stands for analysis of variance. Despite the name, the question it answers is about means. Given three or more groups, it asks "is at least one of these group means different from the rest, more than chance alone would produce?" It does this by comparing how much the group averages spread apart against how much the data wobble within each group.

Why not just run a t-test on every pair? Because each test carries a chance of a false positive, and running many of them stacks those chances up until a "significant" result is likely somewhere by luck alone. ANOVA asks the single overall question once, with one controlled error rate, and only then do you drill into pairs.
When a lab reaches for it

You have a dose series (vehicle, low, medium, high) and one readout, and you want to know whether dose changes the readout at all. Or you have cells from four genotypes and a single growth measurement. Any time the design is "one measured outcome split across three or more conditions," the one-way ANOVA is the starting point.
How to read the result

The Data Hub reports an F statistic , its degrees of freedom , and an overall p-value . The F statistic is the ratio of between-group spread to within-group spread, so a large F with a small p-value means the groups really do differ somewhere. A non-significant ANOVA is a stopping point, if the overall test finds nothing, you do not go fishing for pairwise differences.

blocks.alt: A one-way ANOVA result in the Data Hub comparing four groups, with the source-of-variation table (between groups, within groups, total) reporting SS, DF, MS, F, and the p-value, plus eta-squared and omega-squared effect sizes, and a Multiple comparisons tab.
blocks.caption: The ANOVA table breaks the variation into between-group and within-group parts, and F is the ratio of the two. Eta-squared and omega-squared below it say how much of the spread the grouping explains, and the Multiple comparisons tab holds the post-hoc pairs.

blocks.text: Effect sizes: eta-squared and omega-squared

The F statistic and its p-value tell you whether the groups differ. They do not tell you by how much in a scale you can reason about. For that, the Data Hub reports two effect sizes. Eta-squared is the fraction of total variation the grouping factor explains, computed as the between-groups sum of squares divided by the total sum of squares. An eta-squared of 0.10 means the grouping accounts for 10% of the spread in the data. It is straightforward but slightly upward-biased, because it is calculated on the specific sample rather than the underlying population. Omega-squared corrects for that bias by subtracting an estimate of the degrees-of-freedom penalty from the numerator, which makes it a more honest estimate of the population effect when the sample is small. For large samples the two are nearly identical; for small ones, prefer omega-squared in write-ups. The 95% confidence interval on eta-squared is reported using the noncentral F pivot, so you can see the range of plausible effect sizes.
Post-hoc tests: which pairs differ

A significant ANOVA tells you that some group differs, not which . The post-hoc tests answer that, by comparing each pair of groups while correcting for the fact that you are making many comparisons at once. The Data Hub reports, for each pair, the difference in means , its 95% confidence interval , and an adjusted p-value . "Adjusted" means the p-value has already been corrected for the number of comparisons, so you can read each one at face value.

The Data Hub offers five post-hoc families. Tukey HSD (the default) compares every group to every other using the studentized range, and is the standard choice when you have no pre-specified contrasts. Dunnett compares each treatment to a single named control, which is the right choice when the only comparisons you planned are treatment versus control. Sidak and Bonferroni are all-pairs corrections based on the t distribution, Bonferroni the most conservative, Sidak slightly less so for uncorrelated comparisons. Holm-Sidak is a step-down version of Sidak that gains power by adjusting each comparison based on its rank in the ordered p-value list while enforcing monotonicity. The verdict tells you which pairs cleared the bar in plain words.

blocks.title: Read the post-hoc intervals, not just the stars
blocks.text: A pair can clear significance with a difference that is still tiny. As on the effect sizes page, the confidence interval on each pairwise difference tells you the size and the precision, which is what you actually report.
Two-way ANOVA: two factors at once

A two-way ANOVA is for when two things vary together. Say you cross genotype (wild type vs mutant) with treatment (drug vs vehicle). Now there are three questions, and the two-way ANOVA answers all three.

items: The main effect of genotype : averaging over treatment, do the genotypes differ?
The main effect of treatment : averaging over genotype, does the drug do anything?

The interaction : does the drug's effect depend on the genotype? This is usually the interesting one. A significant interaction means you cannot describe the drug with a single number, its effect is different in the mutant than in the wild type.

blocks.text: The result reports an F statistic and p-value for each of the three rows. Read the interaction first. If it is significant, the two main effects are no longer the whole story and you interpret each combination on its own, often with post-hoc comparisons of the specific cells.
Kruskal-Wallis: nonparametric one-way comparison

When the normality assumption is in doubt or your data are ranks or scores rather than measured quantities, the Kruskal-Wallis test is the nonparametric replacement for the one-way ANOVA. Instead of working on the raw values, it ranks all observations pooled across groups and tests whether the mean ranks of the groups are more spread apart than chance would produce. The result reports an H statistic (corrected for tied ranks) and a chi-square p-value on k minus 1 degrees of freedom. A significant Kruskal-Wallis is followed by Dunn's post-hoc test (Bonferroni-adjusted), which compares mean ranks pairwise. The reported effect size is epsilon-squared , the rank analogue of eta-squared.
Friedman: nonparametric repeated measures

The Friedman test is the nonparametric counterpart of a one-way repeated-measures ANOVA. It is the right choice when the same subjects are measured under each condition but you cannot assume normality. Within each subject (block), observations are ranked across conditions; the test then asks whether the mean ranks differ more across conditions than chance would produce. Like Kruskal-Wallis, a significant Friedman result is followed by Dunn's pairwise post-hoc. Use Friedman when you would otherwise reach for repeated-measures ANOVA but the assumption of a normal within-subject distribution is questionable.
A worked example

Three media give colony counts averaging 50, 65, and 80. The one-way ANOVA returns F = 14.2, p = 0.0003, so the media differ somewhere. The Tukey post-hoc shows medium vs rich differs by 15 colonies (95% CI 6 to 24, adjusted p = 0.002) while minimal vs medium does not clear the bar. You would write "media affected colony count (one-way ANOVA, p = 0.0003); the rich medium yielded 15 more colonies than the medium one (95% CI 6 to 24, adjusted p = 0.002)."

blocks.title: Related designs
blocks.text: If the same subjects are measured under every condition, or your replicates are not independent, you want a repeated-measures or nested design instead, which accounts for that structure rather than treating every point as a fresh independent sample.
ResearchOS validates one-way and two-way ANOVA, the post-hoc families, Kruskal-Wallis, and Friedman against scipy, statsmodels, and R on the transparency page .

entries.title: Contingency tables, odds ratios, and relative risk
entries.summary: When your data are counts in categories rather than measured numbers, responded or did not, mutant or wild type, survived or died, you compare proportions. A contingency table lays those counts out, and a handful of tests and effect sizes tell you whether the categories are linked and how strongly. This page covers chi-square, Fisher exact, logistic regression as a first-class analysis, the odds ratio, and relative risk.
entries.blurb: Chi-square vs Fisher exact, odds ratios (including logistic regression), and relative risk.

blocks.text: When your data are counts in categories rather than measured numbers, responded or did not, mutant or wild type, survived or died, you compare proportions. A contingency table lays those counts out, and a handful of tests and effect sizes tell you whether the categories are linked and how strongly. This page covers chi-square, Fisher exact, logistic regression as a first-class analysis, the odds ratio, and relative risk.
What a contingency table is

A contingency table is a grid of counts. It can be any size: two outcomes by two groups (2x2), three treatment arms by four response categories (3x4), or any R-by-C arrangement. The question is always the same: does the distribution of counts across columns depend on which row you are in, or are the rows and columns independent and any apparent pattern is just chance?
Chi-square and Fisher exact

For any R×C table the Data Hub runs the Pearson chi-square test of independence . It compares the counts you observed against the counts you would expect if the rows and columns were independent. The test is reliable when the expected count in every cell is reasonably large (a common rule of thumb is at least 5); the result reports the minimum expected count so you can check.
For a 2x2 table , the Data Hub always reports all three of the following, regardless of cell counts.

items: Chi-square (Pearson, uncorrected), the standard large-sample statistic.
Chi-square with Yates continuity correction , which subtracts 0.5 from each absolute deviation before squaring, giving a slightly more conservative result for the 2x2 case.

Fisher's exact test , which computes the probability directly from the hypergeometric distribution rather than approximating, so it stays accurate when counts are small. Fisher exact is only computed for 2x2 tables; for larger tables the chi-square is the right test.

blocks.text: All three report a p-value for whether the categories are associated. As elsewhere, that tells you whether there is a link, not how strong it is. For strength, you want the effect sizes below.
Odds ratios

The odds ratio is the workhorse effect size for two-by-two data. Odds are a count ratio, the number with the outcome divided by the number without it. The odds ratio compares the odds in one group against the odds in the other.

items: An odds ratio of 1 means the outcome is equally likely in both groups, no association.
Above 1 means higher odds in the first group. An odds ratio of 3 means the exposed group had three times the odds of the outcome.
Below 1 means lower odds, a protective association.

blocks.text: The Data Hub reports the odds ratio with its 95% confidence interval . If the interval excludes 1, the association is statistically clear, the same call the test's p-value makes, and the interval's width tells you the precision.

blocks.alt: A 2x2 contingency result in the Data Hub, reporting the Yates-corrected and uncorrected chi-square, Fisher's exact p-value, the relative risk, and the odds ratio with its interval, above the observed and expected count tables.
blocks.caption: A 2x2 table. The Data Hub reports the chi-square (Yates-corrected and uncorrected), Fisher's exact p for small counts, the relative risk, and the odds ratio with its 95 percent confidence interval, then shows the observed counts against the counts you would expect if the two factors were unrelated.

blocks.title: Logistic regression is a first-class analysis
blocks.text: Logistic regression is its own analysis in the Data Hub, not just an extension of the contingency table. It predicts a yes/no outcome from a continuous predictor and reports the odds ratio for that predictor, read exactly as above, with its 95% confidence interval and p-value. The practical advantage over a contingency table is that logistic regression works directly on the continuous predictor without binning it, and it reports the X value at which the predicted probability equals 0.5, a useful summary for dose-response-style binary data. When the data are separable (all zeros on one side, all ones on the other) the standard maximum-likelihood estimate diverges; the engine detects this and falls back to Firth's penalized-likelihood correction automatically, keeping the estimates finite and flagging the method in the result. The odds ratio a logistic regression reports is the same quantity as the contingency table odds ratio, but conditioned on the continuous predictor value, just like a coefficient in multiple regression .
Relative risk, and when to use which

Relative risk compares the actual probability of the outcome between groups, not the odds. A relative risk of 2 means the outcome was twice as likely in one group. It is often the more intuitive number, and it is the right one when you sampled groups and then watched for outcomes (a cohort or a trial). The odds ratio is the natural choice for case-control designs and is what logistic regression produces. When the outcome is rare the two numbers nearly coincide; when it is common the odds ratio looks more extreme than the relative risk, so do not read an odds ratio as if it were a risk ratio.
A worked example

Of 50 treated patients, 10 relapsed; of 50 controls, 25 relapsed. Fisher's exact test gives p = 0.002. The odds ratio is 0.27 (95% CI 0.11 to 0.64) and the relative risk is 0.40. You would write "relapse was less frequent on treatment, 20% versus 50% (relative risk 0.40, odds ratio 0.27, 95% CI 0.11 to 0.64, Fisher exact p = 0.002)." The interval not crossing 1 is what makes the association clear.

ResearchOS validates the chi-square, Yates-corrected chi-square, Fisher exact test, odds ratio, relative risk, and logistic regression (including the Firth fallback) against scipy and statsmodels on the transparency page .

entries.title: Correlation and regression
entries.summary: When both of your variables are measured numbers, you usually want to know whether they move together and whether you can predict one from the other. Correlation measures how tightly two things track. Regression fits an actual line you can read a slope off of and use to predict. Multiple regression extends that to several predictors at once. This page covers all three and the trap that sits underneath them.
entries.blurb: How tightly two variables track, fitting a line with a slope you can read, and multiple predictors.

blocks.text: When both of your variables are measured numbers, you usually want to know whether they move together and whether you can predict one from the other. Correlation measures how tightly two things track. Regression fits an actual line you can read a slope off of and use to predict. Multiple regression extends that to several predictors at once. This page covers all three and the trap that sits underneath them.
What correlation measures

Correlation measures how consistently two variables rise and fall together. The common measure is Pearson's r , which runs from -1 to +1. An r near +1 means that when one goes up the other reliably goes up; near -1 means one goes up as the other goes down; near 0 means no straight-line relationship. The closer to the ends, the tighter the cloud of points hugs a line.

The Data Hub reports r , its 95% confidence interval , a p-value for whether the correlation differs from zero, and often r squared , the fraction of the variation in one variable that tracks with the other. An r of 0.7 gives an r squared of about 0.49, so roughly half the variation is shared.

blocks.title: Correlation is not causation, and r is only for straight lines
blocks.text: A strong r says two things track together, not that one causes the other, a lurking third variable can drive both. And Pearson's r only sees straight-line relationships. A perfect U-shaped curve can have an r near zero. Always look at the scatter plot, not just the number.
Spearman correlation

When your data are ranks, scores on an ordinal scale, or visibly non-normal, Spearman's rho is the nonparametric alternative. It works exactly like Pearson's r, but on the rank-transformed data rather than the raw values. The result is a coefficient that runs from -1 to +1 with the same meaning, and the Data Hub reports it with the same 95% confidence interval (Fisher z approximation on the ranks) and a p-value. Because Spearman captures any monotone relationship, not only straight lines, it is less sensitive to outliers and does not assume that the relationship between the two variables is linear.
Fitting a line with simple linear regression

Where correlation gives a single number for tightness, linear regression fits the actual line and hands you its equation. It is the right tool when one variable plausibly drives the other and you want to predict or quantify the relationship, the absorbance you expect at a given concentration, the signal per unit of input.

The result reports the slope with its confidence interval and p-value , the intercept , and r squared for how well the line fits. The slope is the headline, it is how much the outcome changes for each one-unit change in the predictor, in real units. A slope whose confidence interval excludes zero is a relationship you can stand behind.

blocks.alt: A simple linear regression result in the Data Hub, reporting the slope with its standard error and 95 percent confidence interval, the intercept with its interval, and the r-squared.
blocks.caption: The slope is the headline, the change in the outcome for each one-unit change in the predictor, in real units. It comes with its standard error and 95 percent confidence interval, and the r-squared says how much of the variation the line accounts for.

blocks.text: A worked example

You plot fluorescence against protein concentration and fit a line. The slope is 1,250 units per microgram (95% CI 1,180 to 1,320, p 0.0001), with r squared = 0.98. You would write "fluorescence rose 1,250 units per microgram of protein (95% CI 1,180 to 1,320, p 0.0001), and the linear fit explained 98% of the variance (r squared = 0.98)." The tight interval and high r squared together say this is a clean, usable standard curve.
Multiple regression: several predictors at once

Multiple regression predicts one outcome from two or more predictors together. Its real value is that each predictor's effect is estimated while holding the others constant . If yield depends on both temperature and pH, multiple regression tells you the effect of temperature at a fixed pH, separating two influences that a one-at-a-time analysis would tangle together.

The result reports, for each predictor, a coefficient with its confidence interval and p-value , plus an overall r squared and a model-level p-value. Read each coefficient as "the change in the outcome per unit of this predictor, with the other predictors held fixed." A predictor that mattered on its own can fall to non-significance here, which usually means another predictor was carrying the signal all along.

blocks.alt: A multiple regression result in the Data Hub, with a coefficient table reporting the estimate, standard error, t, p-value, 95 percent confidence interval, standardized beta, and VIF for each predictor, plus overall r-squared, adjusted r-squared, and the model F test.
blocks.caption: Each predictor gets its own coefficient, read as the change in the outcome per unit while the other predictors are held fixed. The standardized beta puts the slopes on a common scale, and the VIF column flags predictors that move together, where a value above about 5 to 10 means a coefficient is hard to trust.

blocks.text: The VIF column and multicollinearity

The multiple-regression result includes a VIF (variance inflation factor) for each predictor. The VIF for a given predictor is computed by regressing that predictor on all the other predictors and taking 1 / (1 minus that r-squared). A VIF of 1 means the predictor is completely uncorrelated with the others, and its coefficient is as precisely estimated as the data allow. A VIF above roughly 5 to 10 is a flag that the predictor is redundant with something else in the model, and the coefficient's confidence interval is wider and less stable than it looks in isolation. When two predictors always move together in your setup, the model cannot separate their individual effects, and the coefficients for both get wide, shaky intervals. The fix is more varied data or dropping a redundant predictor, not trusting a narrow-looking coefficient.

blocks.title: Watch for predictors that move together
blocks.text: When two predictors are themselves strongly correlated (temperature and pressure that always rise together in your setup), the model struggles to credit the effect to one or the other, and their individual coefficients get wide, unstable intervals. The fix is more varied data or dropping a redundant predictor, not trusting a knife-edge coefficient.

blocks.title: Related pages
blocks.text: If your outcome is a yes/no category rather than a number, you want logistic regression, a first-class analysis in the Data Hub. It predicts a binary outcome from a continuous predictor and reports an odds ratio with its interval. See the contingency page for how to read that odds ratio. If the relationship is a saturating curve rather than a line, see dose-response curves . The slope and its interval are read the same way as any other effect size, so that page is the foundation here too.
ResearchOS validates Pearson and Spearman correlation, simple regression, and multiple regression (including VIF) against scipy and statsmodels on the transparency page .

entries.title: Dose-response curves
entries.summary: A lot of biology is a saturating S-curve, not a straight line. A drug has little effect at low doses, a steep response through the middle, and a plateau once the system is maxed out. A dose-response fit captures that shape and boils it down to a few numbers you can compare across drugs, days, and labs. This page covers the EC50 and IC50, the sigmoid models that produce them, the Hill slope, and how to compare or share fits.
entries.blurb: EC50 and IC50, the 4PL and 5PL fits, the Hill slope, model comparison, and global fits.

blocks.text: A lot of biology is a saturating S-curve, not a straight line. A drug has little effect at low doses, a steep response through the middle, and a plateau once the system is maxed out. A dose-response fit captures that shape and boils it down to a few numbers you can compare across drugs, days, and labs. This page covers the EC50 and IC50, the sigmoid models that produce them, the Hill slope, and how to compare or share fits.
What a dose-response curve is

You plot response against dose, almost always with dose on a log scale , and fit a smooth S-shaped curve through the points. The curve summarizes the whole experiment with a handful of parameters, the bottom plateau, the top plateau, the dose at the halfway point, and how steeply the response climbs.
EC50 and IC50, the halfway dose

The single most-used number is the dose that produces a half-maximal response. When the response goes up with dose, that is the EC50 (half-maximal effective concentration). When the response goes down, an inhibition, it is the IC50 (half-maximal inhibitory concentration). A lower IC50 means a more potent inhibitor, it takes less drug to get halfway to full effect. This is the number you compare between compounds.

blocks.title: Report the EC50 with its interval, on the log scale
